title: Transcendence Reduction Memo

tan1_is_transcendental.proof_sketch: Hermite-Lindemann: e^i is transcendental (i is algebraic nonzero). e^i = cos(1) + i·sin(1). Both cos(1) and sin(1) transcendental. tan(1) = sin(1)/cos(1), ratio of transcendentals. (Niven: only rational sin values at rational radian multiples of 2π are ±1,±1/2,0)

A_Nesterenko.theorem: π, e^π, Γ(1/4) are algebraically independent (Nesterenko 1996)
A_Nesterenko.applicability: INDIRECT — doesn't directly constrain arg of EML₁ elements
A_Nesterenko.gap: Need to relate EML₁ imaginary parts to π-multiples; not obvious

B_Baker.theorem: Linear forms in logarithms of algebraic numbers are nonzero (Baker 1966)
B_Baker.applicability: INDIRECT — EML₁ Im-parts may not be log of algebraics
B_Baker.gap: tan(1) is transcendental so Baker doesn't directly bound it

C_Schanuel.theorem: Schanuel's conjecture (unproved): trdeg of {z_i, e^{z_i}} ≥ n for ℚ-lin-indep z_i
C_Schanuel.applicability: DIRECT if proved — would show e and tan(1) algebraically independent
C_Schanuel.gap: Unproved conjecture! Cannot use as axiom

D_Structural.theorem: Characterize Im(EML₁) as closed under specific operations
D_Structural.applicability: MOST TRACTABLE — no transcendence theory needed if successful
D_Structural.hypothesis: Im(EML₁) ⊆ ℤ·π + sum of arg values of constructible elements
D_Structural.gap: Requires inductive characterization of Im-parts; S86 is needed for bounds

reduction_chain: ⟸ Im(EML₁) ⊆ ℤ·π (or some restricted set not containing ±Re(z)·tan(1))
⟸ Structural induction on EML₁ depth [requires S86 branch-cut analysis]

literature.Lindemann_1882: π is transcendental; e^α transcendental for algebraic α≠0
literature.Niven_1956: Rational values of trig functions at rational multiples of π
literature.Schanuel_1960s: Conjecture on transcendence degree of exp field extensions

open_question: Does Strategy D close the gap? I.e., can we prove Im(EML₁) ⊆ ℤ·π purely structurally? If yes: i-unconstructibility follows without Schanuel.
